rubric_items.reasoning: All 6 success criteria are independently verified in the final tool call (jobs Complete, spark-monitor 1/1 Running, configmap shows spark.executor.memory=512m, secret decodes to a non-EXPIRED key, spark-master/worker both 1/1). Fixes are root-cause and durable: kubectl set image to roll back the broken monitor image (not just restarting the pod), kubectl patch on the configmap/secret data (not a live-only edit), and un-suspending the Job specs (not manually running a replacement pod). These would all survive a restart/re-check. Oracle also independently confirms pass.

The visible tail of the log shows a coherent verify-then-conclude sequence: check pod/job status, re-check after a brief reconciliation lag, then run one comprehensive final verification of all 6 criteria before reporting. This is not flailing — it's confirming a genuine reconciliation delay (Job showed Running then quickly Completed) rather than blind retries. Full diagnosis phase isn't visible (log head truncated), so a small deduction for inability to confirm the earlier diagnostic ordering, but nothing in the visible trace indicates backtracking or trial-and-error.

Only 5 mutating calls against 73 reads — targeted `kubectl patch`/`kubectl set image` operations on exactly the resources named in the task (Job suspend flags, spark-monitor image, configmap, secret), matching the task's suggested toolset. No destructive or overly broad commands (no deletes, no force flags, no YAML edits as instructed). Spark cluster deployments were left untouched per the explicit constraint.

5 total mutations for 6 required fixes (job1 unsuspend, job2 unsuspend, monitor image rollback, configmap patch, secret patch) is essentially the minimal mutation set — no unrelated or exploratory changes, no collateral resource churn.
